\chapter{Installation}
\label{\detokenize{install:installation}}\label{\detokenize{install::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
This page explains how to install FC\sphinxhyphen{}DFT code.


\section{Requirements}
\label{\detokenize{install:requirements}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
GeomeTRIC

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
PySCF

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
PyAMG

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
PyAMGCL

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
GPU4PySCF (optional)

\end{itemize}


\section{How to install}
\label{\detokenize{install:how-to-install}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Download the lastest version of FC\sphinxhyphen{}DFT from the repository:
\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Clone the repository:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYGZdl{} git clone https://github.com/Yang\PYGZhy{}Laboratory/FC\PYGZhy{}DFT.git
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Change the directory to the cloned repository:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYGZdl{} cd FC\PYGZhy{}DFT
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Install the package using \sphinxtitleref{pip}:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYGZdl{} pip install .
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Change directory to \sphinxtitleref{\$PYTHONPATH/fcdft/lib} and create build directory.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Go into \sphinxtitleref{build} and compile the C shared libraries by \sphinxtitleref{cmake ..} and \sphinxtitleref{make}.

\end{enumerate}

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{Examples}
\label{\detokenize{examples:examples}}\label{\detokenize{examples::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
This chapter introduces practical examples of running FC\sphinxhyphen{}DFT and Poisson\sphinxhyphen{}Boltzmann solvation calculations with the current implementation.


\section{Wide\sphinxhyphen{}Band Limit (WBL) Approximation}
\label{\detokenize{examples:wide-band-limit-wbl-approximation}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
FC\sphinxhyphen{}DFT implements the wide\sphinxhyphen{}band limit (WBL) approximation through the WBL\sphinxhyphen{}Molecule approach, which allows DFT calculations on molecules coupled to electrode contacts. The WBL\sphinxhyphen{}Molecule requires you to specify the imaginary part of the self\sphinxhyphen{}energy (broadening parameter, Γ\_L) and the Fermi level (reference potential).

\sphinxAtStartPar
The self\sphinxhyphen{}energy in WBL is defined as:
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\Sigma_L = \Lambda_L - \frac{i}{2}\Gamma_L S\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
where Λ\_L shifts the energy levels and Γ\_L controls the level broadening (related to the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{broad}} parameter). The applied voltage is incorporated through a potential correction:
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}[\Delta H_L]_{\mu\nu} = \Delta V_b [S]_{\mu\nu}\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
where ΔV\_b = \sphinxhyphen{}(μ \sphinxhyphen{} μ\_ref) is the chemical potential difference from the reference electrode potential.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Currently, the spin\sphinxhyphen{}restricted version (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.wbl.rks}}) is supported. The example below shows how to perform a WBL calculation on methanethiol with a sulfur\sphinxhyphen{}electrode contact:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pyscf}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{gto}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pyscf}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{dft}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{RKS}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Define molecular structure}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{mol} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{gto}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{M}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{atom}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}\PYGZsq{}\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    C    \PYGZhy{}1.718553971    \PYGZhy{}0.000000250    \PYGZhy{}0.626147715}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    H    \PYGZhy{}2.739245971    \PYGZhy{}0.008907250    \PYGZhy{}0.227127715}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    H    \PYGZhy{}1.200493971    \PYGZhy{}0.879491250    \PYGZhy{}0.227127715}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    H    \PYGZhy{}1.215921971     0.888398750    \PYGZhy{}0.227127715}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    S    \PYGZhy{}1.718553971    \PYGZhy{}0.000000250    \PYGZhy{}2.396147715}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    H    \PYGZhy{}2.150082583     0.805150681    \PYGZhy{}2.710667448}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}\PYGZsq{}\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }    \PYG{n}{charge}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{0}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{basis}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{6\PYGZhy{}31g**}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Standard DFT calculation}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{mf} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RKS}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{mol}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{xc}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{pbe}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{mf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{kernel}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} WBL calculation}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{fcdft}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{wbl}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{rks}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{WBLMoleculeRKS}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{wblmf} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{WBLMoleculeRKS}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{mol}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{xc}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{pbe}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{broad}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.01}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{nelectron}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{25.95}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{wblmf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{ref\PYGZus{}pot} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mf}{4.5}  \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Reference potential (e.g., electrode Fermi level) in eV}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{wblmf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{kernel}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{dm} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{wblmf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{make\PYGZus{}rdm1}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}  \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Density matrix at electrode contact}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Poisson\sphinxhyphen{}Boltzmann Solvation Model}
\label{\detokenize{examples:poisson-boltzmann-solvation-model}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.solvent.pbe}} module provides a non\sphinxhyphen{}linear Poisson\sphinxhyphen{}Boltzmann (PB) solver for modeling solvation at electrode/liquid interfaces. Unlike standard PCM models, this implementation can handle complex boundary conditions including Stern layers and is designed for electrochemical systems.

\sphinxAtStartPar
The non\sphinxhyphen{}linear Poisson\sphinxhyphen{}Boltzmann equation is:
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\nabla \cdot (\epsilon(r)\nabla\phi^{\text{tot}}(r)) = -4\pi[\rho^{\text{sol}}(r) + \rho^{\text{ions}}(r)]\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
where ε(r) is the position\sphinxhyphen{}dependent dielectric function, φ\textasciicircum{}tot(r) is the total electrostatic potential, ρ\textasciicircum{}sol(r) is the solute charge density, and ρ\textasciicircum{}ions(r) is the ion charge density. The dielectric function varies smoothly from the SAM (self\sphinxhyphen{}assembled monolayer) to the bulk solvent:
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}\epsilon(z) = \epsilon_{\text{SAM}} + \frac{1}{2}(\epsilon_{\text{bulk}} - \epsilon_{\text{SAM}})\left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{z-a}{\Delta_1}\right)\right]\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Standard Solvation :}

\sphinxAtStartPar
For standard continuum solvation without electrode effects:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pyscf}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{gto}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pyscf}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{dft}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{RKS}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{fcdft}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{solvent}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{pbe}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{PBE}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{pbe\PYGZus{}for\PYGZus{}scf}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Water molecule}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{mol} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{gto}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{M}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{atom}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}\PYGZsq{}\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    O    0.152427064    0.959723218   \PYGZhy{}2.275350162}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    H    0.152427064    1.719060218   \PYGZhy{}1.679307162}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    H    0.152427064    0.200386218   \PYGZhy{}1.679307162}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}\PYGZsq{}\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }    \PYG{n}{charge}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{0}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{basis}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{6\PYGZhy{}31g**}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Standard DFT}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{mf} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RKS}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{mol}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{xc}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{b3lyp}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Setup PBE solvation model}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cm} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{PBE}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{mol}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{cb}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{1.0}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{length}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{15}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{ngrids}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{41}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cm}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{eps} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{l+m+mf}{78.3553}  \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Dielectric constant of water}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cm}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{nelectron} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{mol}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{nelectron}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cm}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{atom\PYGZus{}bottom} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{center}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Solvated DFT calculation}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{solmf} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{pbe\PYGZus{}for\PYGZus{}scf}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{mf}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{cm}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{solmf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{kernel}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Electrochemical Solvation:}

\sphinxAtStartPar
For electrochemical systems with a Stern layer and self\sphinxhyphen{}assembled monolayer (SAM), combine WBL and PBE solvation. The boundary conditions follow the Gouy\sphinxhyphen{}Chapman\sphinxhyphen{}Stern theory.

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pyscf}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{gto}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pyscf}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{dft}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{RKS}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{fcdft}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{wbl}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{rks}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{WBLMoleculeRKS}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{fcdft}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{solvent}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{pbe}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{PBE}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{pbe\PYGZus{}for\PYGZus{}scf}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Methanethiol molecule}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{mol} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{gto}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{M}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{atom}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}\PYGZsq{}\PYGZsq{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    C    \PYGZhy{}1.718553971    \PYGZhy{}0.000000250    \PYGZhy{}0.626147715}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    H    \PYGZhy{}2.739245971    \PYGZhy{}0.008907250    \PYGZhy{}0.227127715}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    H    \PYGZhy{}1.200493971    \PYGZhy{}0.879491250    \PYGZhy{}0.227127715}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    H    \PYGZhy{}1.215921971     0.888398750    \PYGZhy{}0.227127715}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    S    \PYGZhy{}1.718553971    \PYGZhy{}0.000000250    \PYGZhy{}2.396147715}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }\PYG{l+s+s1}{    H    \PYGZhy{}2.150082583     0.805150681    \PYGZhy{}2.710667448}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}\PYGZsq{}\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,}
\PYG{g+gp}{... }    \PYG{n}{charge}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{0}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{basis}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{6\PYGZhy{}31g**}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Step 1: WBL calculation at fixed electrode potential}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{wblmf} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{WBLMoleculeRKS}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{mol}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{xc}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{pbe}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{broad}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{0.01}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{nelectron}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{25.95}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{wblmf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{ref\PYGZus{}pot} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mf}{4.5}  \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Electrode Fermi level}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{wblmf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{kernel}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{dm} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{wblmf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{make\PYGZus{}rdm1}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Step 2: Add solvation with SAM and Stern layer}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cm} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{PBE}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{mol}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{cb}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{1.0}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{length}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{15}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{ngrids}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{41}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{stern\PYGZus{}sam}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mf}{3.0}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cm}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{eps} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{l+m+mf}{78.3553}        \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Water dielectric constant}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cm}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{eps\PYGZus{}sam} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{l+m+mf}{2.284}      \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} SAM dielectric constant}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cm}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{nelectron} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{mol}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{nelectron}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{cm}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{bias} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{wblmf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{bias}    \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Applied potential from WBL calculation}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} FC\PYGZhy{}DFT+ calculation}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{solmf} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{pbe\PYGZus{}for\PYGZus{}scf}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{wblmf}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{cm}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{solmf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{kernel}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{dm0}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n}{dm}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Geometry Optimization}
\label{\detokenize{examples:geometry-optimization}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
FC\sphinxhyphen{}DFT supports analytic gradients for both WBL and solvation models. Geometry optimization can be performed using external optimizers like GeomeTRIC:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pyscf}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{geomopt}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{geometric\PYGZus{}solver}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{optimize}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Optimize structure at fixed electrode potential}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{moleq} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{optimize}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{solmf}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{maxstep}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{100}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Access optimized geometry and energies}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{moleq}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{atom\PYGZus{}coords}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+sa}{f}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Final energy: }\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZob{}}\PYG{n}{moleq}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{e\PYGZus{}tot}\PYG{l+s+si}{:}\PYG{l+s+s2}{.6f}\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZcb{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{ Hartree}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Vibrational Analysis and Thermochemistry}
\label{\detokenize{examples:vibrational-analysis-and-thermochemistry}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
FC\sphinxhyphen{}DFT provides numerical Hessian calculations based on analytic forces. This is important for non\sphinxhyphen{}Hermitian Hamiltonians (WBL) where analytical Hessian is not readily available. The Hessian is constructed using finite differences:
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}H_{ij} = \frac{1}{2\Delta x}\left[\frac{\partial^2 E}{\partial R_i \partial R_j}\right]\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
where the derivatives are obtained from analytic nuclear gradients.

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{fcdft}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{hessian}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{numhess}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{Hessian}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pyscf}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{hessian}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{thermo}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{harmonic\PYGZus{}analysis}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Calculate Hessian using finite differences of analytic gradients}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{hessmf} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{Hessian}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{solmf}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{hess} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{hessmf}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{kernel}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Analyze vibrational frequencies and thermochemistry}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{freq\PYGZus{}info} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{harmonic\PYGZus{}analysis}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{moleq}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{hess}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Save to Molden format for visualization}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{fcdft}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{tools}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{molden}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{dump\PYGZus{}freq}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{dump\PYGZus{}freq}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{moleq}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{freq\PYGZus{}info}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{freq.molden}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{About}
\label{\detokenize{about:about}}\label{\detokenize{about::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Fractional Charge Density Functional Theory (FC\sphinxhyphen{}DFT) is a theory that reformulates open quantum systems in terms of the canonical ensemble. The prototype of FC\sphinxhyphen{}DFT is linear interpolation FC\sphinxhyphen{}DFT (LI\sphinxhyphen{}FC\sphinxhyphen{}DFT), which enforces the Perdew\sphinxhyphen{}Parr\sphinxhyphen{}Levy\sphinxhyphen{}Balduz (PPLB) condition to DFT calculations through linear interpolation and bypasses the delocalization error.


\section{Developers}
\label{\detokenize{about:developers}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Jun\sphinxhyphen{}Hyeong Kim, Duke University
Weitao Yang, Duke University


\section{Citation}
\label{\detokenize{about:citation}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Jun\sphinxhyphen{}Hyeong Kim, Dongju Kim, Weitao Yang, and Mu\sphinxhyphen{}Hyun Baik. Fractional Charge Density Functional Theory and Its Application to the Electro\sphinxhyphen{}inductive Effect. \sphinxstyleemphasis{J. Phys. Chem. Lett.} \sphinxstylestrong{2023}, \sphinxstyleemphasis{14}, 3329\sphinxhyphen{}3334
Jun\sphinxhyphen{}Hyeong Kim and Weitao Yang. Fractional Charge Density Functional Theory Elucidates Electro\sphinxhyphen{}Inductive and Electric Field Effects at Electrochemical Interfaces. \sphinxstyleemphasis{Submitted}


\section{Bug Report and Feature Request}
\label{\detokenize{about:bug-report-and-feature-request}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Please open a thread on the \sphinxhref{https://github.com/Yang-Laboratory/FC-DFT/issues}{Issues} tab.

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{fcdft package}
\label{\detokenize{fcdft:fcdft-package}}\label{\detokenize{fcdft::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
FC\sphinxhyphen{}DFT is a Python package for DFT calculations at electrochemical interfaces.
It combines the wide\sphinxhyphen{}band limit (WBL) approximation for electrode coupling with
continuum solvation models to study molecules at electrode/liquid interfaces under
applied potential.


\chapter{Core Modules}
\label{\detokenize{fcdft:core-modules}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Wide\sphinxhyphen{}Band Limit (WBL)}: Couples molecules to electrode contacts via complex self\sphinxhyphen{}energy
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.wbl.rks}} — Restricted Kohn\sphinxhyphen{}Sham with WBL (RKS + electrode coupling)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.wbl.uks}} — Unrestricted Kohn\sphinxhyphen{}Sham with WBL

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Solvation}: Non\sphinxhyphen{}linear Poisson\sphinxhyphen{}Boltzmann for electrode/liquid interfaces
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.solvent.pbe}} — Poisson\sphinxhyphen{}Boltzmann electrolyte model

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Stern layers and self\sphinxhyphen{}assembled monolayers (SAM) support

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Analytic gradients included

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Gradients}: Analytic nuclear forces for geometry optimization
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.grad.rks}} — Gradients for WBL (RKS)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.grad.uks}} — Gradients for WBL (UKS)

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Hessian}: Numerical Hessians for vibrational analysis
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.hessian.numhess}} — Numerical second derivatives via finite differences

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.hessian.thermo}} — Thermochemistry analysis from Hessians

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Additional}:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.lifcdft}} — Linear\sphinxhyphen{}interaction FC\sphinxhyphen{}DFT for charge analysis

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.jellium}} — Jellium electrode models

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.dft}} — DFT numerics (grids, numerical integration)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fcdft.tools}} — Utilities (Molden export, etc.)

\end{itemize}

Kernel function is the main driver of a method.  Every method should
define the kernel function as the entry of the calculation.  Note the
return value of kernel function is not strictly defined.  It can be
anything related to the method (such as the energy, the wave\sphinxhyphen{}function,
the DFT mesh grids etc.).

\index{get\_mo\_occ\_grad() (in module fcdft.grad.rks)@\spxentry{get\_mo\_occ\_grad()}\spxextra{in module fcdft.grad.rks}}

{\sphinxparam{\DUrole{n}{mf\_grad}}}
{}
\pysigstopsignatures
\sphinxAtStartPar
Per\sphinxhyphen{}MO occupation gradient d(mo\_occ{[}k{]})/d(mu) in a.u.\textasciicircum{}\{\sphinxhyphen{}1\}.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Uses the same quadrature scheme (GAUSS or QUADPACK) as the SCF to compute
the derivative of each MO occupation with respect to the chemical potential
(Fermi level), evaluated at the converged mu.  The spin factor of 2 for
closed\sphinxhyphen{}shell is already included in the returned array.
\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Parameters}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstyleliteralstrong{\sphinxupquote{mf}} \textendash{} WBLMoleculeRKS instance (converged)

\sphinxlineitem{Returns}
\sphinxAtStartPar
d(mo\_occ{[}k{]})/d(mu\_au), real.

\sphinxlineitem{Return type}
\sphinxAtStartPar
mocc\_grad (1D ndarray, shape (nmo,))

\end{description}\end{quote}

\end{fulllineitems}

define the kernel function as the entry of the calculation.  Note the
return value of kernel function is not strictly defined.  It can be

\subsection{fcdft.solvent package}
\label{\detokenize{fcdft.solvent:fcdft-solvent-package}}\label{\detokenize{fcdft.solvent::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Poisson\sphinxhyphen{}Boltzmann solvation model for electrode/liquid interfaces.

\sphinxAtStartPar
This package implements continuum solvation for molecules at electrode surfaces using
a non\sphinxhyphen{}linear Poisson\sphinxhyphen{}Boltzmann (PB) approach. Key features:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Position\sphinxhyphen{}dependent dielectric}: Smooth transition from SAM through Stern layers to bulk

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Ion concentration}: From Boltzmann distribution at the applied potential

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Gouy\sphinxhyphen{}Chapman\sphinxhyphen{}Stern boundary conditions}: Electrode surface treatment

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Analytic gradients}: For geometry optimization

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{GPU acceleration}: Optional gpu4pyscf support for large grids

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The non\sphinxhyphen{}linear PB equation solved is: ∇·(ε(r) ∇φ\_tot) = \sphinxhyphen{}4π{[}ρ\_sol + ρ\_ions(φ\_tot, T){]}

{}
\pysigstopsignatures\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Fitting procedure for (R)ESP atomic charges}
\sphinxAtStartPar
In case of RESP, a penalty function is a hyperbolic form:
penalty funciton = resp\_a sum\_i (q\_i*q\_i \sphinxhyphen{} resp\_b*resp\_b)**0.5 \sphinxhyphen{} resp\_b)

\end{description}


\paragraph{Parameters:}
\label{\detokenize{fcdft.solvent:parameters}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
mol : pyscf.Mole()
grids : np.array (ngrid, 3), grid coordinates
grids\_val : np.array (ngrid), the V at grid point
restraint : bool
\begin{quote}

\sphinxAtStartPar
whether restraint included
\end{quote}
\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{hfree}{[}bool{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
whether hydrogen atoms excluded or included in restraint

\sphinxlineitem{resp\_a}{[}float{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
restraint scale

\sphinxlineitem{resp\_b}{[}float{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
restraint parabola tightness

\sphinxlineitem{maxiter}{[}int{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
maximum number of interactions

\sphinxlineitem{tolerance}{[}float,{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
tolerance for charges in the fitting

\sphinxlineitem{verbose}{[}int{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
print out message

\end{description}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Returns:
qf : list
\begin{quote}

\sphinxAtStartPar
(R)ESP atomic charges
\end{quote}

\end{fulllineitems}

\subsection{fcdft.wbl package}
\label{\detokenize{fcdft.wbl:fcdft-wbl-package}}\label{\detokenize{fcdft.wbl::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Wide\sphinxhyphen{}Band Limit (WBL) approximation for DFT at electrode interfaces.

\sphinxAtStartPar
This package implements DFT calculations on molecules coupled to electrode contacts
using the WBL approximation. The WBL model treats the self\sphinxhyphen{}energy as an imaginary self\sphinxhyphen{}energy.

\sphinxAtStartPar
F = h\textasciicircum{}\{core\} + V\textasciicircum{}\{HF\}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Special treatment (damping, DIIS, or level shift) will be applied to the
Fock matrix if diis and cycle is specified (The two parameters are passed
to get\_fock function during the SCF iteration)
\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Kwargs:}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{h1e}{[}2D ndarray{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Core hamiltonian

\sphinxlineitem{s1e}{[}2D ndarray{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Overlap matrix, for DIIS

\sphinxlineitem{vhf}{[}2D ndarray{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
HF potential matrix

\sphinxlineitem{dm}{[}2D ndarray{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Density matrix, for DIIS

\sphinxlineitem{cycle}{[}int{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Then present SCF iteration step, for DIIS

\sphinxlineitem{diis}{[}an object of \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{SCF.DIIS}} class{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
DIIS object to hold intermediate Fock and error vectors

\sphinxlineitem{diis\_start\_cycle}{[}int{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The step to start DIIS.  Default is 0.

\sphinxlineitem{level\_shift\_factor}{[}float or int{]}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Level shift (in AU) for virtual space.  Default is 0.

\end{description}

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

Calculates the Fermi level by the Newton\sphinxhyphen{}Raphson method.
\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Parameters}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstyleliteralstrong{\sphinxupquote{mf}} (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{WBLMolecule}}) \textendash{} WBLMolecule object.

\sphinxAtStartPar

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstyleliteralstrong{\sphinxupquote{broad}} (\sphinxstyleliteralemphasis{\sphinxupquote{float}}\sphinxstyleliteralemphasis{\sphinxupquote{, }}\sphinxstyleliteralemphasis{\sphinxupquote{optional}}) \textendash{} Broadening parameter. Defaults to None.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstyleliteralstrong{\sphinxupquote{mo\_energy}} (\sphinxstyleliteralemphasis{\sphinxupquote{1D numpy.ndarray}}\sphinxstyleliteralemphasis{\sphinxupquote{, }}\sphinxstyleliteralemphasis{\sphinxupquote{optional}}) \textendash{} MO energies. Defaults to None.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxlineitem{Raises}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstyleliteralstrong{\sphinxupquote{RuntimeError}} \textendash{} Failure of the Gaussian quadrature method.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstyleliteralstrong{\sphinxupquote{RuntimeError}} \textendash{} Infinite Fermi level detected.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstyleliteralstrong{\sphinxupquote{RuntimeError}} \textendash{} Failed to converge in the maximum cycle.

\end{itemize}
